quick_facts.label: Role scope
quick_facts.value: A Dental Surgeon is the professional title used for a dentist trained in surgical and medical treatment of oral-cavity disease. Statutory dental scope includes treatment of conditions of the teeth and jaws, related radiographic work, relevant anaesthesia, and work involving dentures and restorative appliances.

quick_facts.label: Primary entry qualification
quick_facts.value: The usual qualifying route is a recognised Bachelor of Dental Surgery (BDS). The historic BDS regulations prescribe four academic years followed by a compulsory one-year paid rotating internship before award of the degree.

quick_facts.label: Undergraduate admission
quick_facts.value: Under the National Dental Commission Act, 2023, admissions to BDS in institutions governed by the Act are through NEET; common counselling is to be conducted by the designated central authority for All India seats and by state authorities for state-level seats.

quick_facts.label: Current regulator
quick_facts.value: The National Dental Commission (NDC) is the current national regulator; the NDC’s official site states that it came into effect on 19 March 2026, replacing the Dental Council of India.

quick_facts.label: Licensure and registration transition
quick_facts.value: The NDC Act provides that the National Exit Test (Dental) will be the common final-year examination for licence to practise and enrolment in the National or State Register when it becomes operational. The Act preserves the status of dentists already registered before that transition; applicants should check current NDC and State Dental Council notices for the operative process.

quick_facts.label: Postgraduate route
quick_facts.value: MDS is a principal specialist-progression route. The 2017 MDS regulations require a recognised BDS degree and State Dental Council registration for admission; the MDS training period is three academic years.

sections.heading: What a Dental Surgeon does
paragraphs: Dental Surgeons assess and treat diseases, deficiencies and lesions involving teeth, jaws and the oral cavity. Their regulated professional scope also encompasses associated radiographic work, use of anaesthesia in connection with dental treatment, and clinical work connected to fitting, repairing or renewing dentures and restorative appliances.

Professional duties extend beyond procedures: the dental ethics regulations direct dentists to place patient welfare first, use scientifically founded methods, maintain relevant outpatient and inpatient records, and continue to improve professional knowledge and skills. The cited ethics regulations specify that relevant patient records must be preserved for at least three years from the start of treatment.
bullets: Diagnose oral and dental conditions and plan appropriate treatment.
Perform clinical and surgical dental procedures within training, competence and lawful scope.
Undertake or use dental radiographic work and manage restorative or prosthetic treatment as appropriate.
Keep clinical records, communicate professionally with patients, and follow ethical and scientific practice.

sections.heading: Entry route in India
paragraphs: The standard route begins with eligibility for, and admission to, a recognised BDS programme. The NDC Act requires BDS admissions in covered dental institutions to be through NEET, with central and state designated authorities responsible for the respective counselling streams. Admission rules, seat matrices, reservation rules, qualifying scores and counselling dates vary by academic year and counselling authority, so candidates should use the current NTA, MCC and state counselling notices.

Under the continuing BDS course framework, the programme is structured as four academic years plus a compulsory one-year paid rotating internship, with the degree awarded after internship completion. For practice registration and licensing, the sector is in a regulatory transition: the NDC Act establishes NExT (Dental) as the eventual licensing and registration examination, but makes it operational only from a date separately appointed by the Central Government. Applicants should therefore confirm the live requirements with the NDC and relevant State Dental Council before applying for registration or employment.
bullets: Complete Class 12 or equivalent eligibility requirements specified for the BDS admission cycle.
Qualify NEET-UG and participate in the applicable central or state counselling process.
Complete a recognised BDS programme and its required rotating internship.
Meet the registration/licensing requirements in force at the time of graduation and employment.

sections.heading: Core capabilities
paragraphs: The role requires clinical judgement and procedural competence across the regulated scope of dentistry, including oral and jaw conditions, radiographic work, anaesthesia associated with treatment, and restorative or prosthetic care. It also requires patient-centred professional conduct: the ethics regulations emphasise patient welfare, courteous communication, scientifically grounded practice, continuing professional learning and record-keeping.
bullets: Clinical assessment, treatment planning and safe procedural execution.
Manual precision and sound infection-control and documentation practices.
Clear, respectful patient communication and informed professional judgement.
Ethical practice, evidence-based learning and continuing dental education.

sections.heading: Where Dental Surgeons work
paragraphs: Dental Surgeons may work in dental clinics and practices, dental colleges, hospitals and public-health services. Official recruitment examples show dental posts in ESIC hospitals and state/district health missions; these posts commonly require a recognised BDS qualification and valid Dental Council registration. Specific conditions differ by employer: for example, a full-time contractual ESIC engagement may prohibit private practice.
The work can include outpatient care and, where relevant, inpatient care. Accordingly, clinical record duties apply to outpatient and inpatient treatment records where applicable.
bullets: Private dental clinics and group practices.
Hospital dental departments and ESIC facilities.
State, district and rural public-health services.
Dental colleges and academic clinical settings, subject to employer and regulatory requirements.

sections.heading: Career progression and specialisation
paragraphs: After BDS and registration, a Dental Surgeon may build experience in general practice, hospital dentistry, public services or teaching-oriented clinical work. A formal specialist route is MDS. The MDS regulations require a recognised BDS degree and State Dental Council registration for admission, and prescribe three academic years of postgraduate training.

The NDC Act provides that, once NExT (Dental) is operational, it will also form the basis for postgraduate dental admissions. Until that operational transition, the Act specifies NEET-MDS for postgraduate admissions. Candidates should verify the examination and counselling arrangements for the applicable admission year.
bullets: Develop general-practice experience and a clinical patient base.
Move into hospital, public-health, academic or employer-specific dental roles.
Pursue MDS specialisation after meeting the applicable BDS, registration and entrance requirements.
Maintain professional development and comply with current registration obligations.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Pay is employer-, location-, contract- and qualification-dependent rather than nationally uniform. As a dated public-sector example only, a Kerala National Rural Health Mission Dental Surgeon notice published for 2024 listed ₹41,000 per month and required BDS from a recognised university plus Kerala State Dental Council registration. This historical figure is not a current market benchmark and should not be used to estimate an offer in another state, employer or year.

For comparison, some hospital posts require postgraduate qualifications in addition to BDS and registration, illustrating why specialist credentials and role type can materially affect eligibility and compensation. Always rely on the vacancy’s current official pay matrix, consolidated-pay clause and allowances.
bullets: Check whether the posting is permanent, contractual, part-time or fee-for-service.
Compare base pay with stated allowances, service obligations and restrictions on outside practice.
Treat any advertised salary as dated and specific to that vacancy.
